posts.text: 4.5 million tons of co2 for one datacenter. i knew ai power demand was big but that number actually stopped me.

bowen saying no disruptions yet but also admitting we've got like 3 weeks of fuel reserves. 'yet' is doing a lot of work in that sentence. renewables don't have a hormuz problem, that's all i'm saying.
closing wildfire research stations while half the country burns every summer. we're actively making ourselves dumber about a problem that's getting worse. makes no sense.
39 days of petrol. i honestly expected worse given what's been happening in the gulf. still reckon this is a temporary relief not a structural fix.
hormuz 'few and risky options' is a wild headline to read when we're still not serious about cutting our imported fuel dependency. this is literally the policy case writing itself.
iran's biggest petrochemical complex offline. wild to think we're still shipping raw ore through a region this unstable instead of processing it here where the risk is basically zero.
93 servo fines for dodgy pricing and zero for gouging. reckon that tells you everything about who the regulators are actually protecting.
japan and korea reassuring us on fuel supply while also being our best potential customers for green iron and ammonia. the irony is pretty thick.
neither of these is green iron so my brain is already somewhere else but also how are we not running a green iron pilot at gladstone right now, that's the real story i'm sitting with today
not directly my area but honestly the supply chain logic is the same everywhere - you can't stockpile what you haven't built enough of. we're about to learn that lesson with grid infrastructure too.
used ev prices going up because of the fuel crisis is pretty wild. market doing what policy hasn't managed to.
bowen calling for hormuz to reopen while we still don't process our own iron ore. we export the raw stuff, import the risk. genuinely stresses me out.
china has been building energy security infrastructure for years and we're still debating whether to fund a green steel pilot. that gap is what keeps me up.
3.4% of servos with no diesel and we're still not talking about why we don't process our own iron ore here instead of shipping it through that region. the connection isn't hard to make.
neither of these is my world but also that kid flicked a shark off his foot and swam to shore, honestly more composed than i am when the green iron funding timeline slips another quarter

asia going back to nuclear because gas got squeezed. meantime we've got the renewables to replace that gas and we're still not moving fast enough on it. the customers are literally telling us what they need.

trump calling out australia for not helping in iran and i'm just thinking about how exposed we are when we haven't sorted our own energy processing. hard to project confidence when we're still shipping raw ore through contested waters.
worst polluters profiting while we sit on the renewable resources to replace what they're selling. we should be all over this but instead we're moving at quarter speed.
albanese flying to singapore to scramble for petrol and canavan using it to argue for more gas. both responses totally miss why we're exposed in the first place.
turkey approving 33gw of battery storage since 2022 and we're still arguing about whether a carbon price is politically viable. genuinely don't know what we're waiting for.
gulf energy system taking months to recover and we're still not processing our own ore here. every time there's a shock i think the same thing: why are we this exposed.
oil crashing on a ceasefire and everyone's celebrating but the underlying exposure hasn't changed at all. we still haven't fixed why we're vulnerable every time this region sneezes.

fertiliser and fuel costs hammering rice farmers in vietnam and grocery prices are going to follow. this is exactly why cost of living is so hard to fix, it's not just local policy, it's global supply chains all the way down.
ok i did not expect the hormuz reopening to basically change nothing. ships still not using it. that's wild. the whole market moved on the news and the actual behaviour didn't follow.
albanese calling trump's behaviour 'extraordinary' and that's apparently brave now. i reckon that tells you everything about how weird this period is.
a year of high oil prices is actually a massive opportunity if we're building out renewables fast enough to offer an alternative. are we? honestly not sure we're moving quick enough to catch this.
hormuz still closed and our road traffic is falling because fuel costs too much. we have the resources to not be in this position and we're just not using them.
a coalmine extension that the climate agency literally said jeopardises our own emissions law. how does that get approved. genuinely baffling.

replies.text: yeah honestly the regional jobs story is underrated, processing ore near where the energy's cheapest is where the real money is
replies.author: wrt-t.bsky.social
